questionText: Provide details of the environmental risks identified which have had a substantive effect on your organization in the reporting year, or are anticipated to have a substantive effect on your organization in the future.
questionKo: 보고연도 내 귀사에 중대한 영향을 미쳤거나 , 향후 귀사에 중대한 영향을 미칠 것으로 예상되는 환경 리스크 에 대한 세부정보를 입력하십시오 .
issueTags: Water, Ocean, Biodiversity, Plastics, Climate Change, Forests
sectorTags: Financial services, Oil & Gas, Coal, General, Aviation, Capital goods, Transport OEMS - EPM, Chemicals, Metals & mining, Transport services, Steel, Agricultural commodities, Transport OEMS, Energy utilities & power generators, Biodiversity - Mines, Cement, Food, beverage & tobacco, Paper & forestry, Real estate, Construction
simpleHelp: Your response to this question provides data users with an overview of the environmental risks affecting your organization now and in the future. Information on the current and anticipated financial effects of these risks, and your response strategy to manage and mitigate them, are critical elements for guiding investments as well as implementing corporate actions to improve business resilience and environmental stewardship.

guidance.text:
General 
Risks reported should include those which either have had or are anticipated to have a substantive effect on your strategy, business activities, value chain, financial position (e.g. assets and liabilities), financial performance (e.g., revenue or expenditure) and cash flows, irrespective of whether the risk has been responded to or accepted.
If a risk relates to multiple environmental issues (e.g. climate change and water), repeat the risk in a row for each relevant environmental issue and use the same risk identifier (column 2) and provide the same information across these rows. 
Your selection in column 13 "Time horizon over which the risk…" and column 18 "Are you able to quantify the financial effect of the risk?" will determine the appearance of subsequent columns:
If you select "Yes" in column 18 (i.e. you can provide financial figures related to the risk), columns 19 "Financial effect figure in the reporting year (currency)" to column 25 "Explanation of financial effect figure" may be presented depending on the time horizons selected in column 13 "Time horizon over which the risk…".
Risk identifier (column 2) 
Select a unique identifier from the drop-down list to track the risk across subsequent questions and reporting years. Select from Risk1-Risk100 and maintain consistency by using the same identifier for recurring risks. For new risks, use a unique identifier that you have not previously used.
If the risk relates to multiple environmental issues, use the same identifier in each row referring to the risk.
Risk type and primary environmental risk driver (column 4) 
The risk types are based on the TNFD's categories of nature-related physical (acute and chronic) and transition (policy, market, reputation, technology, liability) risks. See the Explanation of terms for more information on the types of risks.
Value chain stage where the risk occurs (column 5) 
Note for financial services companies: The options shown will be driven by the organizational activities you selected in 1.10.
Refer to the "Explanation of Terms" section for definitions of value chain stages.
Risk type mapped to traditional financial services industry risk classification (column 6) 
This column only appears to organizations in the Financial Services sector.
When assessing environmental risks in this column, consider their alignment with your existing organizational framework. Determine how these risks integrate into your traditional industry risk framework, particularly regarding the classification of potential financial effect. If an identified risk corresponds to multiple industry risk categories, select all that apply.
Capital adequacy and risks weighted assets: refers to the minimum amount of capital that must be held by financial institutions in order to reduce the risk of insolvency.
Liquidity risk : occurs when a financial institution cannot meet its short-term debt obligations.
Funding risk: refers to the risk associated with the effect on a project's cash flow from higher funding costs or lack of availability of funds.
Market risk: refers to the possibility of loss resulting from an adverse movement in asset prices.
Credit risk: refers the possibility of a loss resulting from a counterparty's failure to repay a loan or meet contractual obligations.
Insurance risk: refers to the possibility of loss resulting from an event(s) that triggers the insurer to pay claims.
Reputational risk: refers to the risk for negative public perception or to the potential of uncontrollable events to have an effect on a company's reputation.
Policy and legal risk: refers to the possibility that legal action will be taken because of an individual's or corporation's actions, inaction, products, services, or other events.
Systemic risk: the possibility that an event at the company level could trigger severe instability or collapse an entire industry or economy.
Operational risk: refers to the possibility of loss resulting from failed processes, systems, human error or outside influences.
River basin where the risk occurs (column 8) 
This column is only presented for "Water" rows.
Select the appropriate option in this column. If your coverage is not represented in the options provided, use "Other, please specify" to explain it.
Organization-specific description of risk (column 10) 
Provide further contextual information on the risk driver, including more detail on the exact nature, location and/or regulation of the effect concerned, as well as any notable geographic/regional examples.
Include organization-specific details, such as references to activities, programs, products, services, methodologies, or operating locations specific to your organization's business or operations.
% of portfolio value vulnerable to this risk (column 11) 
This column only appears to organizations in the Financial Services sector and if you select "Banking (Bank) portfolio", "Investing (asset owner) portfolio", "Investing (asset manager) portfolio" or "Insurance underwriting portfolio" in column "Value chain stage where the risk occurs". 
Provide the proportion of your total portfolio value based on total assets value (as reported in 1.10) that is vulnerable to this risk. See "Explanation of terms" for a definition of vulnerability.
Likelihood (column 14) 
This column does not appear if "The risk has already had a substantive effect on our organization in the reporting year" is the only selection made in column "Time horizon over which the risk is anticipated to have a substantive effect on the organization".
Magnitude (column 15) 
The magnitude describes the extent to which the effect, if it occurred, would affect your business. You should consider the business as a whole and therefore the magnitude can reflect both the damage that may be caused and the exposure to that potential damage.
For example, two organizations may have identical facilities located on a coast in an area which is vulnerable to sea level rise. However, if organization A relies on that facility for 90% of its production capacity and organization B relies on it for only 40% of its production capacity, the magnitude of a sea level rise effect on organization A will be comparatively higher than that on company B.
It is not possible for CDP to accurately define terms for magnitude as they will vary from organization to organization. For example, a 1% reduction in profits will have different effects on different organizations depending on the profit margins on which they work. Therefore, organizations are asked to determine magnitude on a qualitative scale. Factors to consider include:
The proportion of business units affected;
The size of the effect on those business units;
The dependency of the company on those units; and
The potential for shareholder or customer concern.
If the financial effect has not been assessed by your organization, select "Unknown".
Effect/Anticipated effect of the risk on the financial position, financial performance and cash flows of the organization in the [reporting year/selected future time horizons] (columns 16 & 17) 
These columns appear dependent on selections in column 13 "Time horizon over which the risk is anticipated to have a substantive effect on the organization".
Provide qualitative information about the effect of the risk on your organization, including details on the primary financial effect. You may also detail any other effects of the risks.
Describe how the financial position, financial performance and cash flows could change as a result of the effect of the risk, for example: business stability is weakened and cash flows tighten, which will possibly lead to liquidity issues.
If a quantitative figure cannot be provided in the following columns, explain why here and describe the financial effect in relative terms (e.g., as a percentage relative to a stated or publicly available figure) or provide a qualitative estimate. Otherwise, if you are unable to provide any information to quantify the effect, state "The effect has not been quantified financially".
Examples of reasons for being unable to provide a quantitative figure include: "the effects are not separately identifiable" or "the level of measurement uncertainty is too high, rendering quantitative information about this risk not useful".
In the latter case, this column should also be used to provide a description of the financial effect in relative terms (for example as a percentage relative to a stated or publicly available figure) or give a qualitative estimate of the financial effect. Otherwise, if you have no information about the financial effect, please state "The effect has not been quantified financially". Quantitative information could include:
The line items, totals and subtotals within the related financial statements that are likely to be affected or have been affected by that risk.
Are you able to quantify the financial effect of the risk? (column 18) 
It is acknowledged that figures provided will be estimates and, where possible, assumptions made in arriving at a financial effect figure should be stated in column 26 "Explanation of financial effect figures".
Financial effect/Anticipated financial effect figure […] minimum/maximum (currency) (columns 19-25) 
These columns are only presented if you select "Yes" in column 18 "Are you able to quantify the financial effect of the risk?", corresponding to the time horizons selected in column 13 "Time horizon over which...".
Provide the figure for the financial effect in the reporting year, if relevant. Provide the estimated figure/range for the financial effect per future time horizon, prior to considering any mitigation measures in place. 
This figure should be in the same currency that you selected in 1.2 for all financial information disclosed throughout your response.
Financial figures should reflect the effect of the risk on your organization's financial performance, such as revenue and costs and its financial position, such as assets and liabilities.
For example, for organizations with a medium-term horizon of 3-5 years, a cumulative figure/range should be provided for the years covered by the time horizon.
For risks which have had an effect in the reporting year (column 19 "Financial effect figure in the reporting year (currency)"), consider the following when calculating the financial effect of the risk, alongside any other relevant financial effect of the risk:
For physical risks:
The value of capital expenditure on infrastructure asset repair or replacement as a result of loss and damage,
The percentage increase in insurance costs due to loss and damage,
Capital expenditure on adaption due to the effects of physical risks,
Costs associated with the relocation of operations and suppliers due to the effects of physical risks. 
For policy risks:
A description and value of any fines/penalties received,
A description and value of clean-up costs,
A description and value of costs relating to loss of operating areas,
A description and value of litigation action taken against the organization associated with environmental related issues. 
For market risks:
The costs related to loss of market access,
The costs related to raw material and natural resource price volatility. 
For reputational risks:
The increased costs/loss of revenue due to reputational risks,
The decreased revenues due to reduced demand for products and services and reduced production capacity. 
For technological risks:
Expenditure on R&D for new and alternative technologies related to mitigation and adaptation of risks.
For all risks:
Any other effects from environmental risks which have resulted in increased direct and indirect (operating) costs, increased capital expenditures, increased credit risk, decreased access to capital and asset value, decreased asset useful life leading to write-offs, asset impairment or early retirement of existing assets.
Explanation of financial effect figures (column 26) 
This column is only presented if you select "Yes" in column 18 "Are you able to quantify the financial effects of the risk?".
Explain all financial effect figures across the selected time horizons reported in columns 19-25 and describe the methodology used to determine these figures (or ranges), including:
The approach utilized for calculation;
The calculation method employed;
How the figure relates to the primary effect identified in column 12 "Primary financial effect of the risk";
The numerical values used in the calculation; and
Any underlying assumption influencing the figure.
Cost of response to risk (column 28) 
Provide a quantitative figure representing the cost of your risk response actions. If there are no costs associated with responding to the risk, enter 0.
If an absolute value cannot be provided, report a percentage value by entering 0 in this column and reporting the percentage figure in column 29 "Explanation of cost calculation", including an explanation of the percentage calculation.
This figure should be in the same currency that you selected in question 1.2 for all financial information disclosed throughout your response.
Explanation of cost calculation (column 29) 
Explain the methodology used to calculate the figure for the cost of managing the risk (in column 28), including the numerical values used in your calculation.
If there are no costs associated with responding to the risk, explain how you arrived at a figure of 0, even if the cost is absorbed into business-as-usual activities.
Description of response (column 30) 
Provide details of your organization's response to mitigate, control, transfer or accept the risk, including, if applicable, how the risk has been managed so far and future risk management procedures.
You may also provide the financial effect figures of the residual risk, if applicable.
Describe the effect the response has had/is likely to have.
Include an example of organization-specific risk response actions (e.g., activities, projects, products and/or services).
Report whether the response strategy involves any collective action initiatives or contributes to the progress of a UN Sustainable Development Goal.
Indicate whether your organization anticipates disposing of any asset due to the environmental risk identified.
Note for oil and gas sector organizations: 
Consider the impact of national and international emissions targets on oil and gas product demand. Will these targets lead to a shift towards a less carbon-intensive fuel mix? How will fuel efficiency standards influence demand for fuel?
Identify other regulatory factors affecting demand reduction such as restrictions on flaring or requirements for a certain level of climate-related performance (e.g., requirement for carbon sequestration).
Include these drivers in your response and explain how your portfolio of reserves is evolving in response to these regulatory changes.
Note for Energy utilities & power generators sector organizations: 
Electric utilities and power generators should consider, among other issues:
How national and international targets on demand management might affect electricity demand;
Implications of related policies such as building regulations promoting energy-efficient buildings;
Policies promoting renewable electricity supply and technologies for GHG emissions reduction, e.g. CO2 capture and storage, clean coal technologies and energy storage;
The effects of any emissions trading schemes and compliance with emissions reduction targets, including the analysis of possible scenarios and their effect on your organization;
Consideration of carbon prices on wholesale and retail power prices across different markets in which you operate and the extent to which carbon prices may be reflected in electricity prices in these markets, based on current and anticipated regulatory requirements.
Note for auto and auto component manufacturing organizations: 
Consider the financial and strategic implications of current and planned national, regional, and international policies aimed at increasing automobile fuel efficiency and developing "clean" engines in each market you operate in.
Consider the effect of related environmental policies, such as regulations and standards concerning air quality, alternative fuels usage, and sustainable mobility on your business.
Specifically, you should consider how climate change policy may affect your sales, potential loss of market share, and additional compliance costs. Describe strategies for managing or passing down increased costs throughout the value chain, if applicable.
Note for agricultural sector organizations: 
Report on risks that may affect the revenue associated with the agriculture/forestry, processing/manufacturing and/or distribution. These risks often stem from:
Physical factors, e.g. extreme weather events that disrupt production/supply of raw materials.
Changes in regulation pertaining to agricultural, processing, manufacturing, distribution and/or consumption activities.
Changes in consumer demands and new market trends.
Note for organizations with coal reserves: 
Refer to CDP Technical Note: Guidance for companies with coal reserves for comprehensive information on disclosing demand and stranded asset risk.
Note for financial services companies: 
Report risks that are inherent and could have an effect on your investing, financing, underwriting and/or operational activities, regardless of any prior actions taken to respond to these risks.
Financial effect figures should reflect the direct financial impact on your business, such as the potential write-downs or devaluation of assets, rather than merely portfolio exposure.
Describe risks by sector and/or geography, as relevant.
Consider both physical and transition risks, including the risk of stranded assets. These are assets that are no longer economically viable as a result of climate-related transition or physical risks.
For banks:
Describe concentrations of credit exposure to carbon-related assets.
Disclose environmental risks (transition and physical) in lending and other financial intermediary activities.
For insurance companies:
Consider environmental risks on re-/insurance portfolios, including physical, transition and liability risks;
Consider potential increases in litigation, such as directors and officers (D&O) liability claims; and
Describe environmental risks relevant to your investment portfolio as an asset owner.
Asset owners/asset managers:
Consider environmental risks for each product or investment strategy.
Note for real estate organizations: 
Real estate, being a location-bound and long-term investment, faces significant exposure to environmental risks.
Consider "stranding risks" - where assets may be devaluated or underperform, making them "stranded".
Stranded assets may be subject to write-downs due to:
Demand shifts towards sustainable properties, putting pressure on ‘non-green' assets; and
Increased exposure to acute physical risks (e.g., storms, flooding, wildfires).
Notes for capital goods sector organizations: 
End markets supplied by this sector are subject to increasing regulation and decarbonization targets; from building standards to mandated technologies for power generation. Organizations in this sector are thus indirectly exposed to risks in their value chain, and should consider, among other issues, risks associated with:
Carbon pricing regulation and stricter emissions constraints on products and services;
Shifts in end-market demand away from fossil fuel-dependent technologies.

Otherwise, 만약 you are unable to provide 하나 이상 information to quantify the effect, state "The effect has not been quantified financially". Examples of reasons for being unable to provide a quantitative figure include: "the effects are not separately identifiable" 또는 "the level of measurement uncertainty is too high, rendering quantitative information about this risk not useful". In the latter case, this 열 해야 합니다 also be used to provide a description 의 financial effect in relative terms (for example as a percentage relative to a stated 또는 공개되어 있음 figure) 또는 give a qualitative estimate 의 financial effect. Otherwise, 만약 you have no information about the financial effect, please state "The effect has not been quantified financially". Quantitative information could include: The line items, totals 및 subtotals within the related 재무제표 that are likely to be affected 또는 have been affected by that risk. Are you able to quantify the financial effect 의 risk? (열 18) It is acknowledged that figures 입력 will be estimates and, 여기서 possible, assumptions made in arriving at a financial effect figure 해야 합니다 be stated in 열 26 "설명 of financial effect figures". Financial effect/Anticipated financial effect figure […] 최소/최고 (currency) (열 19-25) These 열 are only presented 만약 you select "예" in 열 18 "Are you able to quantify the financial effect 의 risk?", corresponding to the time horizons 선택 in 열 13 "Time horizon over which...". Provide the figure 에 대한 financial effect in the 보고연도, 만약 관련. Provide the estimated figure/range 에 대한 financial effect per future time horizon, prior to considering 하나 이상 mitigation measures in place. This figure 해야 합니다 be in the same currency that you 선택 in 1.2 for all financial information 공개 throughout your response. Financial figures 해야 합니다 reflect the effect 의 risk on 귀사의 financial performance, such as 매출 및 costs 및 its financial position, such as 자산 및 부채. For example, for organizations with a 중기 horizon of 3-5 years, a cumulative figure/range 해야 합니다 be 입력 에 대한 years covered by the time horizon. For 리스크 which have had an effect in the 보고연도 (열 19 "Financial effect figure in the 보고연도 (currency)"), consider the following 경우 calculating the financial effect 의 risk, alongside 하나 이상 other 관련 financial effect 의 risk: For physical 리스크: The value of 자본지출 on 인프라 asset repair 또는 replacement as a result of loss 및 damage, The percentage increase in insurance costs due to loss 및 damage, 자본지출 on adaption due to the effects of physical 리스크, Costs associated with the relocation of operations 및 공급업체 due to the effects of physical 리스크. For 정책 리스크: A description 및 value of 하나 이상 fines/penalties received, A description 및 value of clean-up costs, A description 및 value of costs relating to 손실 operating areas, A description 및 value of litigation action taken against the organization associated with environmental related issues. For market 리스크: The costs related to 손실 market access, The costs related to raw material 및 natural resource price volatility. For 평판al 리스크: The 증가 costs/손실 매출 due to 평판al 리스크, The decreased 매출s due to reduced demand for 제품 및 서비스 및 reduced production capacity. For technological 리스크: Expenditure on R&D for 신규 및 alternative technologies related to mitigation 및 adaptation of 리스크. For all 리스크: 하나 이상 other effects from environmental 리스크 which have resulted in 증가 direct 및 indirect (operating) costs, 증가 자본지출s, 증가 credit risk, decreased access to capital 및 asset value, decreased asset useful life leading to write-offs, asset impairment 또는 early retirement of existing 자산. 설명 of financial effect figures (열 26) This 열 is only presented 만약 you select "예" in 열 18 "Are you able to quantify the financial effects 의 risk?". Explain all financial effect figures across the 선택 time horizons 보고 in 열 19-25 및 설명 the 방법론 used to determine these figures (or ranges), 포함: The approach utilized for 계산; The 계산 method employed; How the figure relates to the primary effect identified in 열 12 "Primary financial effect 의 risk"; The numerical values used in the 계산; 및 하나 이상 underlying assumption influencing the figure. Cost of response to risk (열 28) Provide a quantitative figure representing the cost of your risk response actions. 만약 there are no costs associated with responding to the risk, enter 0. 만약 an absolute value cannot be 입력, report a percentage value by entering 0 in this 열 및 reporting the percentage figure in 열 29 "비용 계산 설명", 포함 an 설명 의 percentage 계산. This figure 해야 합니다 be in the same currency that you 선택 in 문항 1.2 for all financial information 공개 throughout your response. 비용 계산 설명 (열 29) Explain the 방법론 used to calculate the figure 에 대한 cost of managing the risk (in 열 28), 포함 the numerical values used in your 계산. 만약 there are no costs associated with responding to the risk, explain how you arrived at a figure of 0, even 만약 the cost is absorbed into business-as-usual activities. Description of response (열 30) 세부 정보를 입력 of 귀사의 response to mitigate, control, transfer 또는 accept the risk, 포함, 만약 해당되는 경우, how the risk has been managed so far 및 future 리스크 관리 절차s. You may also provide the financial effect figures 의 residual risk, 만약 해당되는 경우. 설명 the effect the response has had/is likely to have. Include an example of organization-specific risk response actions (e.g., activities, projects, products 및/또는 services). Report whether the response 전략 involves 하나 이상 collective action 이니셔티브 또는 contributes to the progress of a UN Sustainable Development Goal. Indicate whether 귀사 anticipates disposing of 하나 이상 asset due to the environmental risk identified. Note for oil 및 gas sector organizations: Consider the impact of national 및 international emissions 목표 on oil 및 gas product demand. Will these 목표 lead to a shift towards a less carbon-intensive fuel mix? How will fuel efficiency 기준 influence demand for fuel? Identify other regulatory factors affecting demand reduction such as restrictions on flaring 또는 requirements for a certain level of climate-related performance (e.g., requirement for carbon sequestration). Include these drivers in your response 및 explain how your 포트폴리오 of reserves is evolving in response to these regulatory changes. Note for Energy utilities & power generators sector organizations: Electric utilities 및 power generators 해야 합니다 consider, among other issues: How national 및 international 목표 on demand 관리 might affect electricity demand; Implications of related policies such as building regulations promoting energy-efficient buildings; Policies promoting re신규able electricity supply 및 technologies for 온실가스 배출량 reduction, e.g. CO2 capture 및 storage, clean coal technologies 및 energy storage; The effects of 하나 이상 emissions trading schemes 및 compliance with emissions reduction 목표, 포함 the analysis of possible scenarios 및 their effect on 귀사; Consideration of carbon prices on wholesale 및 retail power prices across different 시장 in which you operate 및 the extent to which carbon prices may be reflected in electricity prices in these 시장, based on current 및 anticipated 규제 요구사항. Note for auto 및 auto component manufacturing organizations: Consider the financial 및 strategic implications of current 및 planned national, regional, 및 international policies aimed at increasing automobile fuel efficiency 및 developing "clean" engines in each market you operate in. Consider the effect of related environmental policies, such as regulations 및 기준 concerning air quality, alternative fuels usage, 및 sustainable mobility on your business. Specifically, you 해야 합니다 consider how 기후변화 정책 may affect your sales, potential 손실 market share, 및 additional compliance costs. 설명 strategies for managing 또는 passing down 증가 costs throughout the 가치사슬, 만약 해당되는 경우. Note for agricultural sector organizations: Report on 리스크 that may affect the 매출 associated with the agriculture/forestry, 프로세스ing/manufacturing 및/또는 distribution. These 리스크 often stem from: Physical factors, e.g. extreme weather events that disrupt production/supply of raw materials. Changes in regulation 관련 to agricultural, 프로세스ing, manufacturing, distribution 및/또는 consumption activities. Changes in consumer demands 및 신규 market trends. Note for organizations with coal reserves: Refer to CDP Technical Note: Guidance for companies with coal reserves for comprehensive information on disclosing demand 및 stranded asset risk. Note for 금융 서비스 companies: Report 리스크 that are inherent 및 could have an effect on your investing, financing, underwriting 및/또는 operational activities, regardless of 하나 이상 prior actions taken to respond to these 리스크. Financial effect figures 해야 합니다 reflect the direct financial impact on your business, such as the potential write-downs 또는 devaluation of 자산, rather than merely 포트폴리오 exposure. 설명 리스크 by sector 및/또는 geography, as 관련. Consider both physical 및 transition 리스크, 포함 the risk of stranded 자산. These are 자산 that are no longer economically viable as a result of climate-related transition 또는 physical 리스크. For banks: 설명 concentrations of credit exposure to carbon-related 자산. Disclose environmental 리스크 (transition 및 physical) in lending 및 other financial intermediary activities. For insurance companies: Consider environmental 리스크 on re-/insurance 포트폴리오s, 포함 physical, transition 및 법적 책임 리스크; Consider potential increases in litigation, such as directors 및 officers (D&O) 법적 책임 claims; 및 설명 environmental 리스크 관련 to your investment 포트폴리오 as an asset owner. Asset owners/자산운용사: Consider environmental 리스크 for each product 또는 investment 전략. Note for real estate organizations: Real estate, being a location-bound 및 장기 investment, faces significant exposure to environmental 리스크. Consider "stranding 리스크" - 여기서 자산 may be devaluated 또는 underperform, making them "stranded". Stranded 자산 may be subject to write-downs due to: Demand shifts towards sustainable properties, putting pressure on ‘non-green' 자산; 및 증가 exposure to 급성 물리적 리스크 리스크 (e.g., storms, flooding, wildfires). Notes for capital goods sector organizations: End 시장 supplied by this sector are subject to increasing regulation 및 decarbonization 목표; from building 기준 to mandated technologies for 발전. Organizations in this sector are thus indirectly exposed to 리스크 in their 가치사슬, 및 해야 합니다 consider, among other issues, 리스크 associated with: Carbon pricing regulation 및 stricter emissions constraints on 제품 및 서비스; Shifts in end-market demand away from fossil fuel-dependent technologies.

guidance.text:
The organization discloses environmental risks which have had or are anticipated to have a substantive effect on the organization's strategy, business activities, value chain, financial position (e.g. assets and liabilities), financial performance (e.g., revenue or expenditure) and cash flows. The disclosure per risk includes:
The timeframe, likelihood and magnitude associated with the effects of the risk, 
Current and anticipated effects on the value chain and where these risks are located, 
Whether the risks are physical (acute or chronic) or transition risks (policy, market, reputation, technology, liability),
The current and anticipated financial effects of the risks,
The costs of response to a risk, a description of the response and an explanation of the cost calculation, 
(FS Sector) For financial institutions, environmental risks drivers are mapped to other risk management categories (e.g. credit risk, market risks, operational risk).
(Financial Services sector) Financial institutions assess their portfolio for environmental risks, as well as measure and disclose the current and anticipated financial effect of the risks.

The 공시 per risk includes: The timeframe, likelihood 및 magnitude associated with the effects 의 risk, Current 및 anticipated effects on the 가치사슬 및 여기서 these 리스크 are located, Whether the 리스크 are physical (acute 또는 chronic) 또는 transition 리스크 (정책, market, 평판, 기술, 법적 책임), The current 및 anticipated financial effects 의 리스크, The costs of response to a risk, a description 의 response 및 an 설명 의 비용 계산, (FS Sector) For financial institutions, environmental 리스크 drivers are mapped to other 리스크 관리 categories (e.g. credit risk, market 리스크, operational risk). (금융 서비스 sector) Financial institutions assess their 포트폴리오 for environmental 리스크, as well as measure 및 disclose the current 및 anticipated financial effect 의 리스크.

guidance.text:
Access to capital : cash flows from sources other than an organization's sales and other revenues. It includes cash infusions from investors or securing lines of credit with banks and other lenders. 
Afforestation: establishment of forest through planting and/or deliberate seeding on land that, until then, was not classified as forest, which implies a transformation of land use from non-forest to forest. ( FAO, 2020 ). 
Alternative livestock management practices: livestock management refers to practices that aim to reduce water use and pollution from waste generated in animal husbandry operations e. g by reducing the amount of water needed, evaporation and amount of waste that needs cleaning, feed adequate fiber, reduce the time cattle spend on the yard. 
Animal waste: manure, slurries or other animal by-products derived from animal husbandry operations to be eliminated or discarded as no longer useful or required. 
Aquaculture: the farming of aquatic organisms, including fish, mollusks, crustaceans and aquatic plants. Farming implies some form of intervention in the rearing process to enhance production, such as regular stocking, feeding, protection from predators, etc. Hatchery systems fall under the definition of aquaculture. Farming also implies individual or corporate ownership of the stock being cultivated (adapted from SBTN, 2025 )
Aquaculture improvement project (AIP): a way for a group of stakeholders to come together to tackle high priority sustainability issues by improving the management practices and policies of all farms operating within a specified area defined by the shared use of a waterbody (adapted from FAO, 2022 ).
Better animal waste management practices: practices that aim to minimize pollution and nutrient losses through leaching or runoff from animal manure, slurries and other by-products. This can be the result of practices that reduce the generation of waste, increase its re-use and recycling on and off-farm or the recovery of useful components. 
Bio-based chemicals: bio-based products are wholly or partly derived from materials of biological origin, excluding materials embedded in geological formations and/or fossilized. 
Biodiversity offset: measures taken to compensate for any residual significant, adverse impacts that cannot be avoided, minimized and/or rehabilitated or restored, in order to achieve no net loss or a net gain of biodiversity. Offsets can take the form of positive management interventions such as restoration of degraded habitat, arrested degradation or averted risk, protecting areas where there is imminent or projected loss of biodiversity ( BBOP, 2012 ) 
Capital expenditure : a measure of the value of purchases of fixed assets such as property, buildings, an industrial plant, technology, or equipment. Put differently, CapEx is any type of expense that an organization capitalizes, or shows on its balance sheet as an investment, rather than on its income statement as an expenditure. 
Circular economy plan: a time-bound action plan that clearly outlines how an organization will achieve its strategy to pivot its existing assets, operations, and entire business model towards a trajectory that aligns with a circular economy. Transitioning to a circular economy entails decoupling economic activity from the consumption of finite resources. This means eliminating waste and pollution, as well as circulating products and materials through designing for durability, reuse, remanufacturing, and recycling (adapted from Ellen MacArthur Foundation ). 
Conversion:  loss of a natural ecosystem as a result of its replacement with agriculture or another land use, or due to a profound and sustained change in the natural ecosystem's species composition, structure, or function. 
Deforestation is one form of conversion (conversion of natural forests) 
Conversion includes severe degradation or the introduction of management practices that result in a profound and sustained change in the ecosystem's species composition, structure, or function. 
Change to natural ecosystems that meets this definition is considered to be conversion regardless of whether or not it is legal ( AFi, 2024 ). 
Deforestation:  loss of natural forest as a result of: i) conversion to agriculture or other non-forest land use; ii) conversion to a tree plantation; or iii) severe and sustained degradation. 
Severe and sustained degradation (scenario iii in the definition) constitutes deforestation even if the land is not subsequently used for non-forest land use.
Loss of natural forest that meets this definition is considered to be deforestation regardless of whether or not it is legal.
The definition of deforestation signifies "gross deforestation" of a natural forest where "gross" is used in the sense of "total; aggregate; without deduction for reforestation or other offset" (adapted from AFi, 2024 ).
Direct costs : also known as "costs of goods or services sold". These expenses can be attributed to the manufacture of a particular product or the provision of a particular service. 
Downstream value chain: the activities, sites, resources, relationships, and stakeholders which receive products and/or services from your organization. The downstream value chain varies depending on the nature of the business but may include customers, distributors, logistics providers, and packaging suppliers (Adapted from ESRS, 2023 ). 
Due diligence : a risk management process implemented by an organization to identify, prevent, mitigate, and account for how it addresses environmental and social risks and impacts in its operations, supply chains, and investments (adapted from AFi, 2024 ). 
Ecosystem services: the contributions of ecosystems to the benefits that are used in economic and other human activity ( TNFD, 2025 ) 
Efficient fertilizer and pesticides management (Food, beverage & tobacco and agricultural commodities sectors only): implementation of practices that aim at optimizing the application and minimizing the losses to environment of fertilizer and pesticides or their residues. 
End-of-life infrastructure and technology: establishing and improving infrastructure and technology for end-of-life management of materials and substances, including facilities and technology for their collection, sorting and processing (adapted from European Environmental Bureau ). 
Fishery Improvement Project (FIP): a multi-stakeholder effort to address challenges in a fishery. These projects use the power of the private sector to incentivize improvements in the fishery and seek to make these changes endure through policy change. FIPs always include goals related to environmental sustainability. In recent years, many FIPs have also begun to address other issues, such as human rights, social responsibility, and even financial sustainability ( The Conservation Alliance for Seafood Solutions, 2023 ).
Grievance mechanism: any routinized process through which grievances concerning business-related negative impacts on human rights or the environment can be raised, and remedy can be sought. 
Grievance mechanisms may be State-based or non-State based and they may be judicial or non-judicial ( AFi, 2024 ).
Indirect costs : also known as 'operating cost' or 'overheads'. This generally refers to the essential expenses incurred in order to maintain the business including wages, rent, transport, energy (electricity, fuel, etc.), maintenance, and so on. These expenses cannot be attributed to the manufacture of a particular product or the provision of a particular service - they are standard costs that apply regardless of the volume of goods produced. 
Landscape and jurisdictional initiative:  an on-the-ground collaborative programme to set common goals, take collective action while reconciling different interests, and monitor progress towards improving social, environmental, and economic outcomes at a landscape/jurisdictional scale 
Leakage market: a market in which capital, income or commodities are diverted to non-compliant activities (creating a potential for environmental damage and reputational risk, for example). 
Likelihood : the terms used to describe likelihood are taken from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change's (IPCC) 2013 reports. They are associated with probabilities, indicating the percentage likelihood of the event occurring. It is not necessary for respondents to have calculated probabilities for the risks they are considering, however they can give an indication as to the meaning of the terms: 
Virtually certain: 99-100% probability 
Very likely: 90-100%; 
Likely: 66-100%; 
More likely than not: 50-100%; 
About as likely as not: 33-66%; 
Unlikely: 0-33%; 
Very unlikely: 0-10%; 
Exceptionally unlikely: 0-1%. 
Macro plastics: macro plastics are large plastic waste that are readily visible, with dimensions larger than 5 mm (adapted from Plastic Leak Project ). 
Micro plastics: micro plastics are small plastic particulates below 5 mm in size (adapted from Plastic Leak Project ). 
Multi-stakeholder initiative: an initiative that is governed by different stakeholder groups, including private sector companies and their associations, civil society organizations (e.g., environmental and social NGOs) and possibly farmer organizations, government organizations and knowledge providers ( SAI Platform, 2015 ). 
No-conversion (also referred to as: deforestation- and conversion-free): commodity production, sourcing, or financial investments that do not cause or contribute to the conversion of natural ecosystems.
No-conversion refers to no gross conversion of natural ecosystems, which the Accountability Framework specifies as the appropriate policy and goal on this topic for companies and supply chains. 
The terms "no-conversion" and "conversion-free" are used in favor of "zero-conversion" because "zero" can imply an absolutist approach that may be at odds with the need to sometimes accommodate minimal levels of conversion at the site level in the interest of facilitating optimal conservation and production outcomes (see AFi's definition for minimal level [of deforestation or conversion], 2024 ). (Adapted from AFi, 2024 ). 
No-deforestation (also referred to as: deforestation-free): commodity production, sourcing, or financial investments that do not cause or contribute to deforestation.
No-deforestation refers to no gross deforestation of natural forests, which the Accountability Framework specifies as the appropriate policy and goal on this topic for companies and supply chains. 
In the context of the Accountability Framework, deforestation refers to the loss of natural forest (see definition of deforestation). 
The terms "no-deforestation" and "deforestation-free" are used in favor of "zero deforestation" because "zero" can imply an absolutist approach that may be at odds with the need sometimes to accommodate minimal levels of conversion at the site level in the interest of facilitating optimal conservation and production outcomes (see AFi's definition for minimal level [of deforestation or conversion], 2024 ). (Adapted from AFi, 2024 ). 
Physical risks: 
Acute - occurrence of short term, specific events that change the state of nature. For example, oil spills, forest fires or pests affecting a harvest; 
Chronic - gradual changes to the state of nature. For example, pollution stemming from pesticide use or climate change. 
Recycled content: proportion, by mass, of recycled material in a product. Only pre-consumer and post-consumer materials shall be considered as recycled content (adapted from ISO 14021:2016 ). 
Pre-consumer recycled content: materials diverted from the waste stream during a manufacturing process (adapted from EMF's Global Commitment definitions and reporting guidelines ).
Post-consumer recycled content: material that has been generated by end-users (households, businesses, institutions) after fulfilling its intended purpose, and that would otherwise be disposed of as waste. This material is collected, sorted, and reprocessed through a recycling process to create new products or materials (adapted from EMF's Global Commitment definitions and reporting guidelines 2030 , EUDR (Regulation EU 2023/1115) and ISO 14021:2016 ).
Reforestation: re-establishment of forest through planting and/or deliberate seeding on land classified as forest ( FAO, 2020 ). 
Restoration: the process of assisting the recovery of an ecosystem, and its associated conservation values, that has been degraded, damaged, or destroyed (adapted from SER, 2004 ).
Reuse models:  an established model which creates an organizational, technical and financial system to ensure the possibility of reuse in a closed-loop, open-loop or hybrid system (adapted from Ellen MacArthur Foundation's  Global Commitment definitions and reporting guidelines 2030 ). 
Revenue : gross income arising from the operations of an organization over a period of time. 
Single-use plastic products: a product that is made wholly or partly from plastic and that is not conceived, designed or placed on the market to accomplish, within its life span, multiple trips or rotations by being returned to a producer for refill or re-used for the same purpose for which it was conceived ( Single-Use Plastics Directive, 2019 ). 
Soil conservation practices (Food, beverage & tobacco and agricultural commodities sectors only): practices that deter the soil degradation in quantity or quality, decreasing erosion or nutrient depletion of the soils, i.e. reduced tillage, use of mulching or cover crops. 
Sustainable forest management: The process of managing a forest for achieving the continuous production of desired forest products and services without reducing its inherent values and future productivity, avoiding undesirable social-environmental effects (adapted from ITTO ). 
Sustainable irrigation practices (Food, beverage & tobacco and agricultural commodities sectors only): practices that optimize irrigation water use leading to a decrease in the total amount of water used in irrigation i.e. through more efficient application of water at the field or irrigation system levels. 
Traceability: the ability to follow a product or its components through stages of the supply chain (e.g. production, processing, manufacturing, and distribution) (Adapted from AFi, 2024 ). 
Transition risks: 
Policy - changes in the policy context due to new (or enforcement of existing) policies to create positive impacts on the environment or mitigate negative impacts on the environment. 
Technology - Substitution of products or services with a reduced impact on the environment and/or reduced dependency on the environment. For example, the replacement of plastics with biodegradable containers. 
Market - Changing dynamics in overall markets, including changes in consumer preferences, which arise from changing physical, regulatory, technological and reputational conditions and stakeholder dynamics. For example, the market value of an organization is affected by assets that have decreased in value because there is insufficient freshwater for the production process, or the value of the business' production process is reduced by the emergence of new technologies that require less water to operate. 
Reputation - Changes in perception concerning an organization's actual or perceived environmental impacts, including at the local, economic and societal level. This can result from direct organization impacts, industry impacts and/or impacts of activities upstream and/or downstream in a value chain. 
Liability - Liability risks that arise directly or indirectly from legal claims. As laws, regulations and case law related to an organization's preparedness for environmental action evolves, the incident or probability of contingent liabilities arising from an organization may increase (Adapted from TNFD , 2023).
Tree plantations: a forest predominantly composed of trees established through planting and/or deliberate seeding that lacks key elements of natural forest native to an area such as species composition and structural diversity. 
Tree plantations generally have one or a few tree species and tend to include one or more of the following characteristics (i) planted on cleared land, (ii) harvest regularly, (iii) trees are of even ages, (iv) products from plantation are managed and processed for commercial plantation. 
Tree plantations can consist of tree planted for timber, pulp, non-timber forest products (e.g., rubber latex), or ecosystem services (e.g., soil stabilization). Plantation dominantly by agricultural species (e.g., fruits or oil palm) are considered agriculture, not tree plantations ( AFi, 2024 ) . 
Upstream value chain: (also referred to as "supply chain
" )
the activities, sites, resources, relationships, and stakeholders that provide products and/or services to your organization. This typically involves activities early in the value chain, such as production or development. The upstream value chain varies depending on the nature of the business but may include raw material, component, or equipment suppliers (Adapted from ESRS, 2023 ). 
Water stress (‘areas with'): a concept that considers physical quantity aspects related to water resources, including water availability. As good practice, a water stressed area should be measured at the catchment level as a minimum. Commonly accepted global indicators to assess areas as water stressed and their thresholds for reporting to CDP include: 
Water availability - risk category greater than ‘High risk': 3.4 ( WWF Water Risk Filter) . WWF recommends that users also take into consideration ‘Medium risk': 2.6. This category is based on a multi-model approach which integrates the best available global water scarcity risk indicators: water depletion, baseline water stress, freshwater quantity target and groundwater availability. In addition, WWF recommends that users take into consideration locations facing 'Drought' risk as these can exacerbate risks related to 'Water Availability'.
Baseline water stress - indicator equal to/greater than ‘High': 40-80% ( WRI Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas) . This refers to ratio of total annual water withdrawals to available renewable water supply. 
Baseline water depletion - indicator equal to/greater than ‘High': 50-75% ( WRI Aqueduct Water Risk Atlas) . This refers to the ratio of total annual water consumption to available renewable water supply. 
Vulnerability : the propensity or predisposition to be adversely affected. Vulnerability encompasses a variety of concepts and elements, including sensitivity or susceptibility to harm and lack of capacity to cope and adapt ( IPCC ).
Wild-capture Seafood: marine or freshwater fish or invertebrates that are harvested from the wild (i.e., not farmed). This includes seafood intended for human food consumption, pet food, as well as other uses such as pharmaceuticals and nutraceuticals (adapted from SBTN, 2025 )
guidance.textKo: Access to capital: cash flows from sources other than an 조직의 sales 및 other 매출s. It includes cash infusions from investors 또는 securing lines of credit with banks 및 other lenders. Afforestation: establishment of forest through planting 및/또는 deliberate seeding on land that, until then, was not classified as forest, which implies a transformation of land use from non-forest to forest. (FAO, 2020). Alternative livestock 관리 practices: livestock 관리 refers to practices that aim to reduce 수자원 use 및 pollution from waste generated in animal husbandry operations e. g by reducing the amount of 수자원 needed, evaporation 및 amount of waste that needs cleaning, feed adequate fiber, reduce the time cattle spend on the yard. Animal waste: manure, slurries 또는 other animal by-products derived from animal husbandry operations to be eliminated 또는 discarded as no longer useful 또는 required. Aquaculture: the farming of aquatic organisms, 포함 fish, mollusks, crustaceans 및 aquatic plants. Farming implies some form of intervention in the rearing 프로세스 to enhance production, such as regular stocking, feeding, protection from predators, etc. Hatchery systems fall under the definition of aquaculture. Farming also implies individual 또는 corporate ownership 의 stock being cultivated (adapted from SBTN, 2025) Aquaculture improvement project (AIP): a way for a group of 이해관계자 to come together to tackle high priority sustainability issues by improving the 관리 practices 및 policies of all farms operating within a specified area defined by the shared use of a 수자원body (adapted from FAO, 2022). Better animal waste 관리 practices: practices that aim to minimize pollution 및 nutrient losses through leaching 또는 runoff from animal manure, slurries 및 other by-products. This can be the result of practices that reduce the generation of waste, increase its re-use 및 recycling on 및 off-farm 또는 the recovery of useful components. Bio-based chemicals: bio-based products are wholly 또는 partly derived from materials of biological origin, 제외 materials embedded in geological formations 및/또는 fossilized. Biodiversity offset: measures taken to compensate for 하나 이상 residual significant, adverse impacts that cannot be avoided, minimized 및/또는 rehabilitated 또는 restored, in order to achieve no net loss 또는 a net gain of biodiversity. Offsets can take the form of positive 관리 interventions such as restoration of degraded habitat, arrested degradation 또는 averted risk, protecting areas 여기서 there is imminent 또는 projected 손실 biodiversity (BBOP, 2012) 자본지출: a measure 의 value of purchases of fixed 자산 such as property, buildings, an industrial plant, 기술, 또는 equipment. Put differently, 자본지출(CAPEX) is 하나 이상 type of expense that an organization capitalizes, 또는 shows on its balance sheet as an investment, rather than on its income statement as an expenditure. Circular economy plan: a time-bound action plan that clearly outlines how an organization will achieve its 전략 to pivot its existing 자산, operations, 및 entire business model towards a trajectory that aligns with a circular economy. Transitioning to a circular economy entails decoupling economic activity from the consumption of finite resources. This means eliminating waste 및 pollution, as well as circulating products 및 materials through designing for durability, reuse, remanufacturing, 및 recycling (adapted from Ellen MacArthur Foundation). Conversion: 손실 a natural ecosystem as a result of its replacement with agriculture 또는 another land use, 또는 due to a profound 및 sustained change in the natural ecosystem's species composition, structure, 또는 function. Deforestation is one form of conversion (conversion of natural forests) Conversion includes severe degradation 또는 the introduction of 관리 practices that result in a profound 및 sustained change in the ecosystem's species composition, structure, 또는 function. Change to natural ecosystems that meets this definition is considered to be conversion regardless of whether 또는 not it is legal (AFi, 2024). Deforestation: 손실 natural forest as a result of: i) conversion to agriculture 또는 other non-forest land use; ii) conversion to a tree plantation; 또는 iii) severe 및 sustained degradation. Severe 및 sustained degradation (scenario iii in the definition) constitutes deforestation even 만약 the land is not subsequently used for non-forest land use. 손실 natural forest that meets this definition is considered to be deforestation regardless of whether 또는 not it is legal. The definition of deforestation signifies "gross deforestation" of a natural forest 여기서 "gross" is used in the sense of "total; aggregate; without deduction for reforestation 또는 other offset" (adapted from AFi, 2024). Direct costs: also known as "costs of goods 또는 services sold". These expenses can be attributed to the manufacture of a particular product 또는 the provision of a particular service. 다운스트림 가치사슬: the activities, sites, resources, relationships, 및 이해관계자 which receive products 및/또는 services from 귀사. The 다운스트림 가치사슬 varies depending on the nature 의 business but may include 고객, distributors, logistics providers, 및 packaging 공급업체 (Adapted from ESRS, 2023). 실사: a 리스크 관리 프로세스 implemented by an organization to identify, prevent, mitigate, 및 account for how it addresses environmental 및 social 리스크 및 impacts in its operations, 공급망s, 및 investments (adapted from AFi, 2024). Ecosystem services: the contributions of ecosystems to the benefits that are used in economic 및 other human activity (TNFD, 2025) Efficient fertilizer 및 pesticides 관리 (Food, beverage & tobacco 및 농산물 원자재 sectors only): 이행 of practices that aim at optimizing the application 및 minimizing the losses to environment of fertilizer 및 pesticides 또는 their residues. End-of-life 인프라 및 기술: establishing 및 improving 인프라 및 기술 for end-of-life 관리 of materials 및 substances, 포함 facilities 및 기술 에 대한ir collection, sorting 및 프로세스ing (adapted from European Environmental Bureau). Fishery Improvement Project (FIP): a multi-이해관계자 effort to address challenges in a fishery. These projects use the power 의 private sector to incentivize improvements in the fishery 및 seek to make these changes endure through 정책 change. FIPs always include goals related to environmental sustainability. In recent years, many FIPs have also begun to address other issues, such as human rights, social responsibility, 및 even financial sustainability (The 보전 Alliance for Seafood Solutions, 2023). Grievance mechanism: 하나 이상 routinized 프로세스 through which grievances concerning business-related negative impacts on human rights 또는 the environment can be raised, 및 remedy can be sought. Grievance mechanisms may be State-based 또는 non-State based 및 they may be judicial 또는 non-judicial (AFi, 2024). Indirect costs: also known as 'operating cost' 또는 'overheads'. This generally refers to the essential expenses incurred in order to maintain the business 포함 wages, rent, transport, energy (electricity, fuel, etc.), maintenance, 및 so on. These expenses cannot be attributed to the manufacture of a particular product 또는 the provision of a particular service - they are standard costs that apply regardless 의 volume of goods produced. Landscape 및 jurisdictional 이니셔티브: an on-the-ground collaborative programme to set common goals, take collective action while reconciling different interests, 및 monitor progress towards improving social, environmental, 및 economic outcomes at a landscape/jurisdictional scale Leakage market: a market in which capital, income 또는 commodities are diverted to non-compliant activities (creating a potential for environmental damage 및 평판al risk, for example). Likelihood: the terms used to 설명 likelihood are taken from the Intergovernmental Panel on 기후변화's (IPCC) 2013 reports. They are associated with probabilities, indicating the percentage likelihood 의 event occurring. It is not necessary for respondents to have calculated probabilities 에 대한 리스크 they are considering, however they can give an indication as to the meaning 의 terms: Virtually certain: 99-100% probability Very likely: 90-100%; Likely: 66-100%; More likely than not: 50-100%; About as likely as not: 33-66%; Unlikely: 0-33%; Very unlikely: 0-10%; Exceptionally unlikely: 0-1%. Macro plastics: macro plastics are large plastic waste that are readily visible, with dimensions larger than 5 mm (adapted from Plastic Leak Project). Micro plastics: micro plastics are small plastic particulates below 5 mm in size (adapted from Plastic Leak Project). Multi-이해관계자 이니셔티브: an 이니셔티브 that is governed by different 이해관계자 groups, 포함 private sector companies 및 their associations, civil society organizations (e.g., environmental 및 social NGOs) 및 possibly farmer organizations, government organizations 및 knowledge providers (SAI Platform, 2015). No-conversion (also referred to as: deforestation- 및 conversion-free): commodity production, sourcing, 또는 financial investments that do not cause 또는 contribute to the conversion of natural ecosystems. No-conversion refers to no gross conversion of natural ecosystems, which the Accountability Framework specifies as the appropriate 정책 및 goal on this topic for companies 및 공급망s. The terms "no-conversion" 및 "conversion-free" are used in favor of "zero-conversion" because "zero" can imply an absolutist approach that may be at odds with the need to sometimes accommodate minimal levels of conversion at the site level in the interest of facilitating optimal 보전 및 production outcomes (see AFi's definition for minimal level [of deforestation 또는 conversion], 2024). (Adapted from AFi, 2024). No-deforestation (also referred to as: deforestation-free): commodity production, sourcing, 또는 financial investments that do not cause 또는 contribute to deforestation. No-deforestation refers to no gross deforestation of natural forests, which the Accountability Framework specifies as the appropriate 정책 및 goal on this topic for companies 및 공급망s. In the context 의 Accountability Framework, deforestation refers to the 손실 natural forest (see definition of deforestation). The terms "no-deforestation" 및 "deforestation-free" are used in favor of "zero deforestation" because "zero" can imply an absolutist approach that may be at odds with the need sometimes to accommodate minimal levels of conversion at the site level in the interest of facilitating optimal 보전 및 production outcomes (see AFi's definition for minimal level [of deforestation 또는 conversion], 2024). (Adapted from AFi, 2024). Physical 리스크: Acute - occurrence of short term, specific events that change the state of nature. For example, oil spills, forest fires 또는 pests affecting a harvest; Chronic - gradual changes to the state of nature. For example, pollution stemming from pesticide use 또는 기후변화. Recycled content: proportion, by mass, of recycled material in a product. Only pre-consumer 및 post-consumer materials shall be considered as recycled content (adapted from ISO 14021:2016). Pre-consumer recycled content: materials diverted from the waste stream during a manufacturing 프로세스 (adapted from EMF's Global Commitment definitions 및 reporting guidelines). Post-consumer recycled content: material that has been generated by end-users (households, businesses, institutions) after fulfilling its intended purpose, 및 that would otherwise be disposed of as waste. This material is collected, sorted, 및 re프로세스ed through a recycling 프로세스 to create 신규 products 또는 materials (adapted from EMF's Global Commitment definitions 및 reporting guidelines 2030, EUDR (Regulation EU 2023/1115) 및 ISO 14021:2016). Reforestation: re-establishment of forest through planting 및/또는 deliberate seeding on land classified as forest (FAO, 2020). Restoration: the 프로세스 of assisting the recovery of an ecosystem, 및 its associated 보전 values, that has been degraded, damaged, 또는 destroyed (adapted from SER, 2004). Reuse models: an established model which creates an organizational, technical 및 financial system to ensure the possibility of reuse in a closed-loop, open-loop 또는 hybrid system (adapted from Ellen MacArthur Foundation's Global Commitment definitions 및 reporting guidelines 2030). 매출: gross income arising from the operations of an organization over a period of time. Single-use plastic products: a product that is made wholly 또는 partly from plastic 및 that is not conceived, designed 또는 placed on the market to accomplish, within its life span, multiple trips 또는 rotations by being returned to a producer for refill 또는 re-used 에 대한 same purpose for which it was conceived (Single-Use Plastics Directive, 2019). Soil 보전 practices (Food, beverage & tobacco 및 농산물 원자재 sectors only): practices that deter the soil degradation in quantity 또는 quality, decreasing erosion 또는 nutrient depletion 의 soils, i.e. reduced tillage, use of mulching 또는 cover crops. Sustainable forest 관리: The 프로세스 of managing a forest for achieving the continuous production of desired forest 제품 및 서비스 without reducing its inherent values 및 future productivity, avoiding undesirable social-environmental effects (adapted from ITTO). Sustainable irrigation practices (Food, beverage & tobacco 및 농산물 원자재 sectors only): practices that optimize irrigation 수자원 use leading to a decrease in the total amount of 수자원 used in irrigation i.e. through more efficient application of 수자원 at the field 또는 irrigation system levels. Traceability: the ability to follow a product 또는 its components through stages 의 공급망 (e.g. production, 프로세스ing, manufacturing, 및 distribution) (Adapted from AFi, 2024). Transition 리스크: 정책 - changes in the 정책 context due to 신규 (or enforcement of existing) policies to create positive impacts on the environment 또는 mitigate negative impacts on the environment. 기술 - Substitution of products 또는 services with a reduced impact on the environment 및/또는 reduced dependency on the environment. For example, the replacement of plastics with biodegradable containers. Market - Changing dynamics in overall 시장, 포함 changes in consumer preferences, which arise from changing physical, regulatory, technological 및 평판al conditions 및 이해관계자 dynamics. For example, the market value of an organization is affected by 자산 that have decreased in value because there is insufficient fresh수자원 에 대한 production 프로세스, 또는 the value 의 business' production 프로세스 is reduced by the emergence of 신규 technologies that require less 수자원 to operate. 평판 - Changes in perception concerning an 조직의 actual 또는 perceived environmental impacts, 포함 at the local, economic 및 societal level. This can result from direct organization impacts, industry impacts 및/또는 impacts of activities upstream 및/또는 downstream in a 가치사슬. 법적 책임 - 법적 책임 리스크 that arise directly 또는 indirectly from legal claims. As laws, regulations 및 case law related to an 조직의 preparedness for environmental action evolves, the incident 또는 probability of contingent 부채 arising from an organization may increase (Adapted from TNFD, 2023). Tree plantations: a forest predominantly composed of trees established through planting 및/또는 deliberate seeding that lacks key elements of natural forest native to an area such as species composition 및 structural diversity. Tree plantations generally have one 또는 a few tree species 및 tend to include one 또는 more 의 following characteristics (i) planted on cleared land, (ii) harvest regularly, (iii) trees are of even ages, (iv) products from plantation are managed 및 프로세스ed for commercial plantation. Tree plantations can consist of tree planted for timber, pulp, non-timber forest products (e.g., rubber latex), 또는 ecosystem services (e.g., soil stabilization). Plantation dominantly by agricultural species (e.g., fruits 또는 oil palm) are considered agriculture, not tree plantations (AFi, 2024). 업스트림 가치사슬: (also referred to as "공급망 ") the activities, sites, resources, relationships, 및 이해관계자 that provide products 및/또는 services to 귀사. This typically involves activities early in the 가치사슬, such as production 또는 development. The 업스트림 가치사슬 varies depending on the nature 의 business but may include raw material, component, 또는 equipment 공급업체 (Adapted from ESRS, 2023). 수자원 stress (‘areas with'): a concept that considers physical quantity aspects related to 수자원 resources, 포함 수자원 availability. As good practice, a 수자원 stressed area 해야 합니다 be measured at the catchment level as a 최소. Commonly accepted global indicators to assess areas as 수자원 stressed 및 their thresholds for reporting to CDP include: 수자원 availability - risk category 초과 ‘High risk': 3.4 (WWF 수자원 Risk Filter). WWF recommends that users also take into consideration ‘Medium risk': 2.6. This category is based on a multi-model approach which integrates the best available global 수자원 scarcity risk indicators: 수자원 depletion, baseline 수자원 stress, fresh수자원 quantity 목표 및 ground수자원 availability. In addition, WWF recommends that users take into consideration locations facing 'Drought' risk as these can exacerbate 리스크 related to '수자원 Availability'. Baseline 수자원 stress - indicator equal to/초과 ‘High': 40-80% (WRI Aqueduct 수자원 Risk Atlas). This refers to ratio of total annual 수자원 withdrawals to available re신규able 수자원 supply. Baseline 수자원 depletion - indicator equal to/초과 ‘High': 50-75% (WRI Aqueduct 수자원 Risk Atlas). This refers to the ratio of total annual 수자원 consumption to available re신규able 수자원 supply. Vulnerability: the propensity 또는 predisposition to be adversely affected. Vulnerability encompasses a variety of concepts 및 elements, 포함 sensitivity 또는 susceptibility to harm 및 부족 capacity to cope 및 adapt (IPCC). 자연산 어획 수산물: marine 또는 fresh수자원 fish 또는 invertebrates that are harvested from the wild (i.e., not farmed). This includes seafood intended for human food consumption, pet food, as well as other uses such as pharmaceuticals 및 nutraceuticals (adapted from SBTN, 2025)

criteria.criteria:
ROUTE A)
For rows where 'Climate change' is selected in column 'Environmental issue the risk relates to':
To be eligible for Awareness points, either 'Yes, only within our direct operations' selected in column ‘Environmental risks identified' in row ‘Climate change' in question 3.1 AND ‘Direct operations' selected in column ‘Value chain stage where the risk occurs' in question 3.1.1
OR
'Yes, only in our upstream/downstream value chain (excluding direct operations)' selected in column ‘Environmental risks identified' in row ‘Climate change' in question 3.1 AND Either ‘Upstream value chain' OR ‘Downstream value chain' selected in column ‘Value chain stage where the risk occurs' in question 3.1.1
OR
‘Yes, both in direct operations and upstream/downstream value chain' selected in column ‘Environmental risks identified' in row ‘Climate change' in question 3.1 AND ‘Direct operations' AND Either ‘Upstream value chain' OR ‘Downstream value chain' selected in column ‘Value chain stage where the risk occurs' in question 3.1.1
Points will be awarded per completed row in proportion to the number of rows disclosed (excluding both columns 'Cost of response to risk' AND 'Explanation of cost calculation') as follows:
Any option excluding 'Unknown' selected in the following columns:
- 'Likelihood of the risk having an effect within the anticipated time horizon' (as applicable)
- 'Magnitude'
A maximum of 7/7 points is available for this route.
OR
NOT APPLICABLE ROUTE)
'Climate change' NOT selected in column 'Environmental issue the risk relates to' in any row - 0/0 points
A maximum of 0/0 points is available for this route.
criteria.criteriaKo: 경로 A) For 행 여기서 '기후변화' is 선택 in 열 'Environmental issue the risk relates to': To be 획득 가능 for 인지 점, either '예, only within our 직접 운영사업장' 선택 in 열 ‘식별된 환경 리스크' in 행 ‘기후변화' in 문항 3.1 그리고 ‘직접 운영사업장' 선택 in 열 ‘가치사슬 stage 여기서 the risk occurs' in 문항 3.1.1 또는 '예, only in our upstream/다운스트림 가치사슬 (제외 직접 운영사업장)' 선택 in 열 ‘식별된 환경 리스크' in 행 ‘기후변화' in 문항 3.1 그리고 Either ‘업스트림 가치사슬' 또는 ‘다운스트림 가치사슬' 선택 in 열 ‘가치사슬 stage 여기서 the risk occurs' in 문항 3.1.1 또는 ‘예, both in 직접 운영사업장 및 upstream/다운스트림 가치사슬' 선택 in 열 ‘식별된 환경 리스크' in 행 ‘기후변화' in 문항 3.1 그리고 ‘직접 운영사업장' 그리고 Either ‘업스트림 가치사슬' 또는 ‘다운스트림 가치사슬' 선택 in 열 ‘가치사슬 stage 여기서 the risk occurs' in 문항 3.1.1 점수가 부여됩니다 per 완성 행 in proportion to the number of 행 공개 (제외 both 열 'Cost of response to risk' 그리고 '비용 계산 설명') as follows: 하나 이상 option 제외 'Unknown' 선택 in the following 열: - 'Likelihood 의 risk having an effect within the anticipated time horizon' (as 해당되는 경우) - 'Magnitude' 이 경로에서 획득 가능한 최고점은 7/7점입니다. 또는 NOT APPLICABLE 경로) '기후변화' NOT 선택 in 열 'Environmental issue the risk relates to' in 하나 이상 행 - 0/0 점 이 경로에서 획득 가능한 최고점은 0/0점입니다.

criteria.criteria:
ROUTE A)
For rows where 'Climate change' is selected in column 'Environmental issue the risk relates to':
The following conditions must be met to be eligible for Management points:
- Full Awareness points awarded (per row) AND
- 4.5/4.5 Management points awarded in question 2.2.2
Points awarded for one complete row (excluding columns 'Cost of response to risk' AND 'Explanation of cost calculation') as follows:
i) All of the following met - 1 point
'Yes' selected in column 'Are you able to quantify the financial effect of the risk?' AND
Figure provided in the following combination of columns (where applicable as per selection(s) in column 'Time horizon over which the risk is anticipated to have a substantive effect on the organization'):
- 'Anticipated financial effect figure in the short-term - minimum (currency)'
- 'Anticipated financial effect figure in the medium-term - minimum (currency)'
- 'Anticipated financial effect figure in the long-term - minimum (currency)'
AND
Figure greater than 0 provided in the following combination of columns (where applicable as per selection(s) in column 'Time horizon over which the risk is anticipated to have a substantive effect on the organization'):
- 'Financial effect figure in the reporting year (currency)'
- 'Anticipated financial effect figure in the short-term - maximum (currency)'
- 'Anticipated financial effect figure in the medium-term - maximum (currency)'
- 'Anticipated financial effect figure in the long-term - maximum (currency)'
ii) Figure provided in column 'Cost of response to risk' - 0.5 points
iii) Column 'Explanation of cost calculation' completed - 0.5 points
A maximum of 2/2 points is available for this route.
One row scored.
OR
NOT APPLICABLE ROUTE)
'Climate change' NOT selected in column 'Environmental issue the risk relates to' in any row - 0/0 points
A maximum of 0/0 points is available for this route.
criteria.criteriaKo: 경로 A) For 행 여기서 '기후변화' is 선택 in 열 'Environmental issue the risk relates to': The following conditions 해야 합니다 be met to be 획득 가능 for 관리 점: - 인지에서 최고점 획득 (per 행) 그리고 - 4.5/4.5 관리 점 awarded in 문항 2.2.2 점 awarded for one complete 행 (제외 열 'Cost of response to risk' 그리고 '비용 계산 설명') as follows: i) All 의 following met - 1 점 '예' 선택 in 열 'Are you able to quantify the financial effect 의 risk?' 그리고 Figure 입력 in the following combination of 열 (여기서 해당되는 경우 as per 선택(s) in 열 'Time horizon over which the risk is anticipated to have a substantive effect on the organization'): - 'Anticipated financial effect figure in the 단기 - 최소 (currency)' - 'Anticipated financial effect figure in the 중기 - 최소 (currency)' - 'Anticipated financial effect figure in the 장기 - 최소 (currency)' 그리고 Figure 초과 0 입력 in the following combination of 열 (여기서 해당되는 경우 as per 선택(s) in 열 'Time horizon over which the risk is anticipated to have a substantive effect on the organization'): - 'Financial effect figure in the 보고연도 (currency)' - 'Anticipated financial effect figure in the 단기 - 최고 (currency)' - 'Anticipated financial effect figure in the 중기 - 최고 (currency)' - 'Anticipated financial effect figure in the 장기 - 최고 (currency)' ii) Figure 입력 in 열 'Cost of response to risk' - 0.5 점 iii) 열 '비용 계산 설명' 완성 - 0.5 점 이 경로에서 획득 가능한 최고점은 2/2점입니다. One 행 scored. 또는 NOT APPLICABLE 경로) '기후변화' NOT 선택 in 열 'Environmental issue the risk relates to' in 하나 이상 행 - 0/0 점 이 경로에서 획득 가능한 최고점은 0/0점입니다.

change.change_summary:
Row "Ocean" has been added.
Added functionality to stop the selection of contradictory datapoints. New dropdowns added 'Farmed seafood / Aquaculture', 'Wild-capture seafood' and 'Other, please specify'.
New dropdowns added: 
• 'Enlargement of low vessel speed zones [O only]', 
• Introduction of regulatory noise pollution limits
• Stakeholder conflicts concerning water resources at a basin/catchment level [W only] 
• Algal bloom [W, O only]
• Disease/pests 
• Invasive species
• Mass coral bleaching event [O only]
• Ocean deoxygenation [O only]
• Over exploitation of species
Modified dropdown options:
• Non-compliance with regulation and legislation 
• Increased partner and stakeholder concern and/or negative feedback from partners and stakeholders feedback 
• Negative press coverage related to support of projects or activities with negative impacts on the environment (e.g. GHG emissions, deforestation & conversion, water stress, excessive bycatch)
• Change in land-use/freshwater/ocean use 
• Increased levels of environmental pollutants in freshwater or marine water bodies [W, O only]
• Uncertainty about commodity origin and/or legality [F, O only]
• Exclusion of vulnerable and marginalized stakeholders (e.g., informal workers) 
Appearance of multiple dropdowns changed to appear to more environmental themes:
• Introduction of regulatory standards for previously unregulated contaminants [W, O, P only]
• Protected area designation 
• Regulation of discharge quality/volumes [W, O only]
• Inability to increase yield of existing production areas [F, O only]
• Transition to water intensive, low-carbon energy sources [C only]
• Lack of availability and/or increased cost of certified sustainable material 
• Lack of availability and/or increased cost of raw materials 
• Lack of availability and/or increased cost of recycled or renewable content [ P only]
• Pollution incident 
• Rupture of tailings dams [W-MM, CO only] 
• Toxic spills [W, O only]
• Changing wind patterns [C, O only]
• Declining ecosystem services [F, W, B only]
• Declining water quality [W only]
• Groundwater depletion [W only]
• Heat stress [C, F only]
• Increased ecosystem vulnerability [F, W, B only]
• Increased levels of macro or microplastic leakage to air, soil, freshwater and/or marine bodies 
• Lack of availability and/or increased cost of certified sustainable material
• Lack of availability and/or increased cost of raw materials 
• Lack of availability and/or increased cost of recycled or renewable content [P only]
• Uncertainty about commodity origin and/or legality [F, O only]
• Operations in or adjacent to areas important for biodiversity 
• Seasonal supply variability
